If Both Can Make API Calls, Why Do Developers Still Debate Them?
One of the first things you’ll learn while building web applications is that your frontend and backend need a way to communicate.
Imagine you’re building an e-commerce website.
When a user opens the homepage, the frontend needs to ask the server:

• Show me all products.

• Show today’s offers.

• Is this user logged in?
Or maybe you’re building a weather app.
The frontend asks:
“What’s the weather in Mumbai today?”
The server responds with the requested data.
This communication happens through HTTP requests.
In JavaScript, there are two popular ways to make these requests:

• Fetch API

• Axios
At first glance, they seem almost identical.
Both send requests.
Both receive responses.
Both work with APIs.
So naturally, beginners ask:
“If both do the same thing, why does Axios even exist?”
That’s exactly what we’ll answer in this article.
Instead of simply comparing features, we’ll understand how each one works, why Axios became popular, and when you should actually choose one over the other.

Before Learning Fetch or Axios…
Let’s understand the real problem.
Imagine you’re ordering food online.
You open Swiggy.
You select a burger.
You press Order.
What happens next?
Your app sends a request to the restaurant.
"I want one Veg Burger."The restaurant processes the request.
Then it sends back a response.
"Your order has been accepted."Frontend and backend communicate in exactly the same way.
The browser sends a request.
The server sends a response.
This request-response cycle happens thousands of times every second across the internet.
Both Fetch and Axios simply help JavaScript perform this communication.

What is Fetch?
Fetch is the browser’s built-in API for making HTTP requests.
The important word here is built-in.
You don’t install it.
You don’t download a package.
Modern browsers already include Fetch.
That means you can immediately write:
fetch("https://api.example.com/users");No installation.
No configuration.
Nothing extra.
It’s already available.
Think of Fetch like the calculator app on your phone.
You didn’t install it.
It came with the phone.

Your First Fetch Request
fetch("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users")
.then(response => response.json())
.then(data => {
console.log(data);
});Let’s understand every line.
Step 1
fetch(url)This sends the HTTP request.
At this point, the browser says:
“Hey server, can you send me the users?”

Step 2
The server replies.
But the response isn’t actual JSON yet.
Instead, Fetch gives us a Response object.
Response
├── status
├── headers
├── body
└── metadataThat’s why we write:
response.json()This converts the response body into a JavaScript object.

Step 3
Now we finally get the actual data.
console.log(data);
Why Do We Need response.json()?
This confuses almost every beginner.
Suppose the server sends:
{
"name":"John",
"age":25
}You may think Fetch gives this object directly.
It doesn’t.
Instead, Fetch first gives a Response object.
You have to tell it:
“Please convert the body into JSON.”
That’s exactly what this does.
response.json()Without it,
console.log(response);prints something like:
Response {
body: ...
headers: ...
status: 200
}Not your actual data.

What is Axios?
Axios is a JavaScript library created to make HTTP requests easier.
Unlike Fetch, Axios is not built into JavaScript.
You have to install it.
npm install axiosThen import it.
import axios from "axios";Now you can make requests.
const response = await axios.get("/users");console.log(response.data);Notice something?
There’s no
response.json()Axios automatically converts JSON into a JavaScript object.
That’s one reason many developers prefer it.

Why Was Axios Created?
Years ago, browsers didn’t fully support Fetch.
Developers had to use something called XMLHttpRequest, which was verbose and harder to work with.
Axios solved many of those problems by providing a cleaner and more consistent API.
Even though Fetch is now widely supported, Axios remains popular because it includes many conveniences out of the box.

Fetch vs Axios: Understanding the Differences
1. Installation
Fetch
Already available in modern browsers.
fetch(...)Axios
Needs installation.
npm install axiosIf you’re working in Node.js, Axios is also straightforward to use because it behaves consistently across environments.

2. JSON Parsing
Fetch
const response = await fetch(url);const data = await response.json();Two steps.
Axios
const response = await axios.get(url);const data = response.data;One step.
Axios handles JSON parsing automatically.

3. Error Handling
This is one of the biggest differences.
Suppose your server returns:
404 Not FoundFetch
Fetch does not reject the promise for HTTP errors like 404 or 500.
It only rejects for network failures (for example, if there’s no internet connection).
So this code can still enter the try block:
const response = await fetch(url);You need to check the status yourself:
if (!response.ok) {
throw new Error("Something went wrong");
}Many beginners forget this step, which can lead to bugs.

Axios
Axios automatically rejects the promise for non-2xx HTTP responses.
try {
await axios.get(url);
}
catch(error){
console.log(error.response.status);
}This often results in cleaner error-handling code.

4. Request Timeout
Sometimes a server takes too long to respond.
You might want to stop waiting after a few seconds.
Axios makes this easy:
axios.get(url,{
timeout:5000
});With Fetch, there’s no built-in timeout option. You usually combine it with AbortController to cancel the request manually.

5. Request Cancellation
Modern Fetch supports cancellation using AbortController.
const controller = new AbortController();fetch(url,{
signal: controller.signal
});controller.abort();Axios also supports request cancellation using the standard AbortController in recent versions.

6. Interceptors
Imagine every request needs an authentication token.
Without interceptors, you might repeat this in every API call.
Authorization: Bearer tokenAxios lets you define this once.
axios.interceptors.request.use(...)Now every request automatically includes the token.
You can also intercept responses, which is useful for handling expired sessions, logging, or global error messages.
Fetch doesn’t include this feature. Developers usually build their own wrapper function around Fetch when they need similar behavior.

7. Upload Progress
Suppose you’re uploading a large video.
You want to display:
10%30%70%100%Axios has built-in support for upload progress in browsers.
With Fetch, tracking upload progress is much more involved and isn’t as straightforward.

A Side-by-Side Example
Fetch
const response = await fetch("/users");if (!response.ok) {
throw new Error("Request failed");
}const data = await response.json();console.log(data);
Axios
const response = await axios.get("/users");console.log(response.data);The Axios version is shorter because it handles several details automatically.

Performance: Is Axios Faster?
This is a common misconception.
In most applications, the network is the slow part, not Fetch or Axios.
If an API takes 800 milliseconds to respond, neither library will magically make it arrive in 300 milliseconds.
The difference in execution time between Fetch and Axios is tiny compared to the time spent waiting for the server.
For most real-world projects, performance shouldn’t be the deciding factor.

Which One Is Easier for Beginners?
Many people find Axios easier because:

• Automatic JSON parsing

• Simpler error handling

• Cleaner syntax

• Helpful built-in features
Fetch, on the other hand, teaches you more about how HTTP actually works. You write a little more code, but you also gain a better understanding of what’s happening behind the scenes.

Which One Do Companies Use?
The answer is simple:
Both.
Some teams prefer Fetch because it doesn’t add another dependency and modern browsers support it well.
Others choose Axios because features like interceptors, automatic error handling, and consistent behavior can improve developer experience.
The choice usually depends on the project’s needs and the team’s preferences rather than one library being objectively better.

When Should You Use Fetch?
Fetch is a good choice when:

• You’re building a small project.

• You don’t want an extra dependency.

• You only need simple GET or POST requests.

• You’re learning how HTTP requests work.

• You want to rely on built-in browser features.

When Should You Use Axios?
Axios is a strong choice when:

• Your application makes many API calls.

• Authentication tokens need to be attached to every request.

• You want centralized request and response handling.

• You want simpler error handling.

• You need request timeouts or upload progress.

• You’re working on medium or large production applications.

Which One Is Better?
There isn’t a universal winner.
The better choice depends on the project.
If your goal is to learn JavaScript and understand the fundamentals, start with Fetch. Because it’s built into the browser, you’ll naturally learn concepts like response objects, status codes, and JSON parsing.
Once you’re comfortable with those basics, learning Axios becomes much easier because you’ll understand the problems it solves rather than treating it as a magic tool.
For many production applications, especially those with authentication, multiple APIs, and shared request logic, Axios often provides a smoother developer experience.

A Practical Learning Path
If you’re just getting started, here’s a path that works well:

• Learn how HTTP requests and responses work.

• Practice making GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE requests with Fetch.

• Understand status codes, headers, request bodies, and error handling.

• Learn async/await thoroughly.

• Then move to Axios and explore features like interceptors, request cancellation, timeouts, and reusable API clients.
By following this order, you’ll understand why Axios exists instead of simply knowing how to use it.

Final Thoughts
Developers often ask, “Should I learn Fetch or Axios?”
The better question is:
“Do I understand how HTTP communication works?”
Once that foundation is clear, switching between Fetch and Axios becomes easy. They are simply different tools for the same job.
If you’re learning web development, begin with Fetch. It helps you build a strong understanding of the browser and the request-response cycle. After that, explore Axios to see how experienced teams reduce repetitive code and simplify larger applications.
In the end, great developers aren’t defined by whether they use Fetch or Axios. They’re defined by understanding what’s happening every time their application talks to a server.
